text.title: Agentic brainstorming antipatterns
text.summary: Four common failure modes in agent-driven ideation: circular scoring bias, premature convergence, semantic clustering errors, and positional bias in large lists.

antipattern.description: Using the same agent that generated the ideas to also dot-vote or rank them.
antipattern.reason: The generating agent has higher confidence in ideas it produced with higher certainty in the first pass. Self-scoring compounds this into a self-reinforcing ranking with no independent judgment.

rule.statement: Scoring must be done by a separate agent or human. The generating agent provides the list; a fresh agent or human applies the scoring rubric.
rule.rationale: Separation of generation and evaluation is the most important architectural rule in agentic brainstorming pipelines.

antipattern.description: Including evaluation language ("good idea because...", "this works better than option B") in the generation prompt.
antipattern.reason: The model self-censors and produces fewer, safer ideas. The generation pass becomes a mixed pass that produces a filtered list masquerading as a brainstorm.

rule.statement: Generation prompt must contain only: problem statement, constraints, format instructions, and quantity target. No evaluation language.
rule.rationale: Strict format enforcement (numbered list only, no explanations) prevents evaluation language from contaminating output even when the model defaults to justification.

antipattern.description: Accepting cluster assignments without human review, particularly when two conceptually distinct approaches share domain vocabulary.
antipattern.reason: Cosine similarity clusters by word overlap. "Automate onboarding email sequence" and "Automate onboarding task assignment" may cluster together despite being architecturally different solutions.

antipattern.description: Passing a list of 50+ ideas to a scoring prompt in a single call.
antipattern.reason: Ideas in positions 1-15 receive systematically higher scores than identical ideas in positions 35-50 due to positional bias in LLM attention. The first batch is not comparable to the last batch.

rule.statement: Chunk idea lists into batches of 25 for scoring. Score each batch against the rubric independently, then compare top-scored ideas across batches in a final pass.
rule.rationale: Chunking equalizes positional treatment. Cross-batch comparison in the final pass is a manageable 25-item set with no positional artifact.
